post_content: Autobus Cycling Club is a mixed-gender cycling club with a clear women-relevant side in Toronto built around road rides. It’s run as a non-profit, so the emphasis is on community, consistency, and getting people out riding together.

Quick Cranks is a women-focused cycling club in Ottawa built around mountain bike, road, gravel, and cyclocross rides. Expect a community-minded atmosphere and a clear sense that riding together is the point. It keeps women riders at the center of the experience, which gives the whole thing a welcoming feel.
Brampton Cycling Club is a mixed-gender cycling club with a clear women-relevant side in Brampton built around road rides. Founded in 1972, it brings a long local history to the ride.

Caledon Cycling Club is a mixed-gender cycling club with a clear women-relevant side in Caledon built around mountain bike, road, and gravel rides. Expect a community-minded atmosphere and a clear sense that riding together is the point. That regular cadence gives it the feel of a real club, not just a page on the internet.

Common Empire is an Ottawa cycling community that runs recurring group rides and events, including a weekly women’s-only ride designed to connect women cyclists in the city. The broader program mixes road rides, gravel adventures, coffee spins, and even bikepacking, with a friendly community feel and a strong emphasis on getting outside together. It’s less a single race team and more a welcoming ride network built around shared adventures on two wheels.

Tour de Cafe is a women-focused novice road ride run by New Hope Community Bikes in Hamilton. It’s a recurring social ride with a strong beginner-friendly and cycling-safety emphasis, ending with a coffee stop and a welcoming community feel. The program is designed to help women build confidence on the bike in a supportive group setting.

LapDogs Cycling Club is a Toronto-based, volunteer-run cycling club that has been rolling since 2003, with social rides, clinics, and race support across road, gravel, mountain biking, cyclocross, and track. Its mentor and first-ride chaperone offerings specifically aim to welcome more women and gender-diverse riders, making the club especially relevant to women looking for a supportive entry point into group riding. The overall vibe is inclusive, community-minded, and built around riding together and having fun.

Saddle Sisters of High Park is a lively Toronto ride community that grew out of a small pandemic-era group and now brings together more than 300 riders. It centers women and welcomes trans, femme, and non-binary cyclists, with organized morning rides, beginner-friendly sessions, and social-paced outings that keep the group rolling together. The mix includes road rides, gravel/trail sessions, skills practice, and occasional race-team activity, all built around a supportive no-drop feel.

Women’s Cycling Network is a Toronto-based community built to help women feel safer and more confident on bikes. Founded in 2017, it brings together new riders and experienced cyclists through advocacy, education, group rides, and city-exploring outings, with a strong focus on empowerment and access. The site also highlights bike-match support that helps women get a bike to ride.

NCR Spoke Sisters is a women-focused road cycling club in Ottawa, built for female-identifying cyclists who want a safe, friendly, and encouraging place to ride. The club says it welcomes beginners through advanced riders, runs no-drop rides, and offers programs, clinics, and challenges to help members build skills, confidence, and community. ([spokesisters.ca](https://www.spokesisters.ca/))

Femmes & Friends is a women-centered cycling collective in Toronto that brings riders together for welcoming, no-drama bike outings. Its site describes a community built for women of all ages, abilities, identities, incomes, and mobilities, with recurring social rides and events like a Femme Friendly Bike Fair. The vibe is friendly, inclusive, and built around making biking feel joyful and accessible rather than competitive.

Wild Bettys is a women-focused mountain biking club built for riders of all ages and abilities. It offers weekly Tuesday evening rides in Toronto’s Don Valley, plus weekend destination rides, beginner-friendly sessions, and skills clinics. The club also fields a race team and leans into a supportive, fun, non-competitive community vibe.

Hustle Pro Cycling is a real Toronto-based cycling team with a strong community-facing side. Its official site highlights “Women’s Outdoor Rides,” described as bringing Toronto’s women’s cycling community together each spring and summer, alongside road racing, gravel events, and fundraising rides. The overall vibe is grassroots, competitive, and community-minded rather than a one-off event page.

Ottawa Women’s Cycling Club is a volunteer-run, not-for-profit cycling club for women in Ottawa-Gatineau. Its mission is to bring women together for rides, events, training, and even racing, with an inclusive community feel for riders of different ages and experience levels. The club offers regular road and gravel rides, plus clinics and special events, including a Gravel Weekend ride.

Ottawa Bicycle Club’s Women’s Time Trials is a long-running women-focused program that dates back to 1989, created to encourage the development of female cyclists. It’s a Tuesday evening road time-trial series for riders ranging from recreational participants to serious racers, with a volunteer-driven, community-minded feel and an optional post-ride social gathering. The club also lists women’s-only rides and includes women’s time trials in its membership benefits, reinforcing that this is a real recurring women-centered cycling program.

MTB Chicks is an informal women-focused mountain bike ride group within the Ottawa Mountain Bike Association. It offers supportive, confidence-building rides for women of all ages and skill levels, with regular outings around Ottawa that range from technical local trails to cross-country rides and social get-togethers.
